lists.title: Xhosa A1 Grammar
lists.description: Foundations: subject concords, the first noun classes, copulatives, negation, locatives, having.

lists.title: Xhosa A2 Grammar
lists.description: Possessive/object/adjective concords, recent past, future, demonstratives.

lists.title: Xhosa B1 Grammar
lists.description: Negation across tenses, subjunctive, remote past, relative clauses.

lists.title: Xhosa C2 Grammar
lists.description: Mastery: amaqhalo, hlonipha, and the particles and greetings of real conversation.

points.title: Subject concords and the present (ndi-, u-, ba-…)
points.explanation:
Every verb starts with a subject concord: ndi- (I), u- (you / he-she cl.1), si- (we), ni- (you all), ba- (they).

The long present adds -ya- when nothing follows the verb: Ndiyafunda (I am studying); with an object the -ya- drops: Ndifunda isiXhosa (I study Xhosa).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.hint: to learn + 'I' concord (no -ya- before an object)

points.title: Noun classes 1/2: um-/aba- (people)
points.function: Talk about people, one or many
points.explanation:
Xhosa nouns carry class prefixes.

Class 1/2 is people: umntu/abantu (person/people), umfundi/abafundi (student/s), umntwana/abantwana (child/ren).

The class drives agreement on everything that follows.
points.culture_note: The class system (about 15 classes) is the engine of the whole language — one class pair at a time is the way in.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.hint: class 1a takes oo- in the plural

points.title: Noun classes 5/6 and 7/8
points.function: Use the ili-/ama- and isi-/izi- families
points.explanation: Class 5/6: i(li)-/ama- — ilizwe/amazwe (country/ies), iqanda/amaqanda (egg/s). Class 7/8: isi-/izi- — isitya/izitya (dish/es); isi- also names languages: isiXhosa, isiNgesi (English).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Noun classes 9/10: in-/izin-
points.explanation:
Class 9/10 covers many everyday nouns and animals: inja/izinja (dog/s), indlu/izindlu (house/s), incwadi/iincwadi (book/s — long ii- plural).

Their agreements use i-/zi-: Inja iyatya (The dog is eating), Izinja ziyatya.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Identifying copulatives (ngu-, yi-)
points.explanation:
'It is X' prefixes the noun: ngu- for class 1/1a (people/names) — NguThemba (It's Themba), ngumfundi (he's a student); yi- for class 9 — yincwadi (it's a book); li-, si-, etc. for other classes.

Negation prefixes asi-: asingumfundi (he is not a student).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Present negative (andi-…-i)
points.explanation: Wrap the verb in a negative concord and final -i: ndiyafunda → andifundi (I don't study), uyafuna → akafuni (he doesn't want — u- becomes aka-), bayasebenza → abasebenzi (they don't work).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.hint: to speak + negative, I (final vowel changes)

drills.translation: I don't live in Cape Town.

points.title: Locatives (e-…-ini)
points.explanation:
Places are made with e- (and often -ini): isikolo → esikolweni (at school), indlu → endlwini (in the house), iKapa → eKapa (in Cape Town).

Ndihlala eKapa (I live in Cape Town), Ndiya esikolweni (I'm going to school).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: I am going to town tomorrow.
The children play in the park.

points.title: Questions (ntoni, bani, phi)
points.explanation:
Question words follow the verb: Ufuna ntoni? (What do you want?), Ngubani igama lakho? (What/who is your name?), Uhlala phi? (Where do you live?).

Yes/no questions just use rising intonation, or open with Ingaba: Ingaba uyeza? (Are you coming?).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: To have: -na-
points.explanation:
'Have' fuses -na- with the noun: ndi + na + incwadi → Ndinencwadi (I have a book), Unemali (you have / she has money), Banabantwana (they have children).

Negative: Andinamali (I have no money).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Possessive concords (yam, wam, bam…)
points.explanation:
The possessive agrees with the THING possessed: incwadi yam (my book, cl.9 ya-), umntwana wam (my child, cl.1 wa-), isitya sam (my dish, cl.7 sa-), abantwana bam (my children, cl.2 ba-).

The endings -m (my), -kho (your), -khe (his/her) stay constant.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Recent past (-ile)
points.explanation:
The recent past ends in -ile when the verb closes the clause: Ndifundile (I studied / have studied), Bafikile (They have arrived). Before an object the short form -e is used: Ndibone umfundi?

— commonly Ndibonile in isolation; keep -ile clause-final at this level.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Future (-za ku-)
points.explanation:
The future combines -za (come) with the infinitive: Ndiza kufunda (I will study), Uza kufika ngomso (She will arrive tomorrow), Siza kuthetha (We will talk).

Colloquially it contracts (ndizo-/ndizaku-).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: I am going to work tomorrow.
We are going to go to Cape Town.
drills.hint: infinitive 'to go' after the future marker

drills.translation: The children are going to play.
What are you going to do tomorrow?

points.title: Object concords
points.function: Point the verb at its object
points.explanation: The object can live inside the verb as a concord before the stem: Ndiyayifunda (incwadi) — I am reading it (the book, cl.9 -yi-); Ndiyambona (umntu) — I see him (cl.1 -m-); Ndiyazithanda (izinja) — I love them (cl.10 -zi-).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: The book? I am reading it.
The water? I am drinking it.

points.title: Adjective concords (omhle, entle…)
points.explanation:
Adjectives take a concord from the noun's class: umntwana omhle (a beautiful child), abantwana abahle, incwadi entle (a nice book, cl.9), isitya esihle.

The stem (-hle beautiful, -khulu big, -ncinci small) is constant; the concord changes.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Demonstratives (lo, le, eli…)
points.explanation:
Demonstratives agree with the class: lo mntu (this person, cl.1), le ncwadi (this book, cl.9), eli qanda (this egg, cl.5), esi sitya (this dish, cl.7), aba bantwana (these children, cl.2).

'That' lengthens: loo, leyo, elo…

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Negative past and future
points.explanation: Negative past uses a-…-anga: Andifundanga (I didn't study), Abafikanga (they didn't arrive). Negative future inserts -zi: Andizi kufunda (I will not study), Akazi kufika (she won't arrive).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: Themba is not going to arrive tomorrow.
drills.hint: future negative, he — the short -zi form

drills.translation: We are not going to play tomorrow.
drills.hint: infinitive 'to play' after the negative future

points.title: Subjunctive (-e) and ma- (let's)
points.explanation: The subjunctive swaps final -a for -e: Masifunde! (Let's study!, ma- + si- + -e), Ndifuna ukuba ufunde (I want you to study), Yiza ukuze sithethe (Come so that we talk).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: I want you to speak well.
What do you want me to do?

points.title: Remote past (a-…-a)
points.explanation:
The remote past fuses the concord with -a-: ndi + a → nda-: Ndahlala eKapa (I lived in Cape Town, back then), Wafunda eRhini (He studied in Makhanda), Bathetha (they spoke, long ago).

It is the storytelling past.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: I lived in Cape Town long ago.
I worked in Johannesburg long ago.
Mum lived in East London long ago.
We studied in Makhanda long ago.

drills.hint: to grow up + remote past, he

points.title: Relative clauses (o-…-yo)
points.explanation: Relatives wrap the verb in a relative concord and -yo: umntwana ofundayo (the child who studies), abantwana abafundayo, incwadi endiyifundayo (the book that I am reading — with the object concord inside).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: the book that I am reading

drills.hint: to love + relative frame with 'I' and a class-15 object

points.title: Passive (-wa)
points.function: Say what is done, without the doer
points.explanation: Insert -w- before the final vowel: funda → fundwa, thetha → thethwa: Incwadi iyafundwa (The book is being read), IsiXhosa sithethwa eKapa (Xhosa is spoken in Cape Town), Umsebenzi wenziwe (The work has been done).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: Xhosa is spoken in Cape Town.
The children are loved by their mother.

points.title: Causative (-isa)
points.explanation: -isa turns 'do' into 'cause to do': funda (learn) → fundisa (teach), sebenza (work) → sebenzisa (use — 'make work'): Utitshala ufundisa abantwana (The teacher teaches the children), Ndisebenzisa ifowuni (I use a phone).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: Mum is putting the child to sleep.
I am showing the book to the students.
The teachers teach the children Xhosa.

points.title: Applicative (-ela)
points.function: Do something for or to someone
points.explanation: -ela adds a beneficiary or direction: bhala (write) → bhalela (write to/for): Ndibhalela umama ileta (I am writing mother a letter); sebenza → sebenzela (work for): Usebenzela inkampani (She works for a company).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: I am writing a letter to mother.
She works for a big company.
Mum cooks food for the children.
I am buying a book for my friend.
He sends money to his mother.
We are singing a song for the baby.

points.title: Reciprocal (-ana)
points.function: Say people do things to each other
points.explanation: -ana makes the action mutual: thanda → thandana (love each other): Bayathandana (They love each other); bona → bonana (see each other): Sobonana ngomso (We'll see each other tomorrow); ncedana (help each other).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: See you tomorrow! (we shall see each other)
People greet each other in the morning.

points.title: Conditions with ukuba
points.explanation:
Ukuba opens conditions and 'that' clauses: Ukuba uyafunda, uza kuphumelela (If you study, you will pass), Ndiyazi ukuba uyeza (I know that you are coming).

'When(ever)' uses xa with the participial: Xa ndifunda… (When I study…).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: If you study, you will pass.
I know that you are coming.
If you want to succeed, study.
I don't know when he is going to arrive.
If it rains, we will stay in the house.
I am glad that you have arrived.

points.title: Noun classes 11, 14, 15 (ulu-, ubu-, uku-)
points.explanation: Class 11 ulu- covers long/thin things and some mass nouns (ulwimi language, ubisi milk) with lu- concords; class 14 ubu- carries abstracts (ubomi life, ubuhle beauty) with bu- concords; class 15 uku- is the infinitive as a noun (ukutya food/eating) with ku- concords.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: We love the beauty of this country.

points.title: Absolute pronouns (mna, wena, yena…)
points.explanation:
The concords carry the grammar, so the absolute pronouns exist for emphasis and contrast: mna (me), wena (you), yena (he/she), thina (we), nina (you all), bona (they).

After na- they fuse: nam (with me), nawe (with you), naye (with him/her).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: As for ME, I am working today.
And YOU, what do you like?
As for ME, I like coffee.
And YOU, where do you live?

points.title: Persistive -sa- (still / no longer)
points.function: Say what's still going on — and what stopped
points.explanation: -sa- after the subject concord means 'still': ndisafunda (I am still studying), usalele (she is still asleep). The negative flips it to 'no longer': andisahlali apha (I no longer live here), akasaseli (he no longer drinks).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: The children are still playing outside.
Do you still live in Cape Town?

drills.hint: to live + negative still = no longer, I
to drink + negative still = no longer, he

points.title: Counting with concords (ezimbini, bangaphi)
points.function: Count things — and make the number agree
points.explanation:
Number stems -nye, -bini, -thathu, -ne, -hlanu take the noun's concord: abantu ababini (two people), iincwadi ezimbini (two books), iminyaka emihlanu (five years).

-ngaphi asks how many with the same agreement: bangaphi abantu?

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

points.title: Instrumental nga- (ngemoto, ngesiXhosa)
points.function: Say by, with, in, and on-a-day with one prefix
points.explanation: Nga- + noun makes by/with/in adverbs, coalescing with the noun's vowel: ngemoto (by car), ngesiXhosa (in Xhosa), ngesandla (by hand), ngoMvulo (on Monday), ngovuyo (with joy).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: They go to work by train.

points.title: When-clauses: xa + participial
points.explanation: Xa opens a when/while-clause and the verb goes participial — no -ya-, and class 1 subjects shift u- → e-, class 2 ba- → be-: xa ndifunda (when I study), xa utitshala ethetha (when the teacher speaks), xa abantwana bedlala (when the children are playing).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: When I study, I listen to music.
When it rains, we stay in the house.
I will be glad when you arrive.
When we have time, we eat together.
He listens when the teacher speaks.
drills.hint: to speak — participial class 1 (u- becomes e-)

drills.translation: When the children are playing, we are happy.
drills.hint: to play — participial class 2 (ba- becomes be-)

points.title: Potential -nga- (ndingakunceda — may I?)
points.explanation:
-nga- after the subject concord makes ability or permission: ndingakunceda? (may I help you?), ungahamba (you may go), ungafunda (you can learn).

The negative uses -nako + infinitive: andinako ukuza (I can't come).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: 'May I help you?' asked the shopkeeper.
You can learn Xhosa in one year.
We can all manage to help.
May I ask him a question?

points.title: Stative -eka / -akala (kufuneka, iyabonakala)
points.function: Say what's visible, audible, necessary, available
points.explanation: The stative extensions turn doing into being-able-to-be-done: bonakala (be visible/evident), vakala (be heard/felt), fumaneka (be available), lahleka (get lost) — and the everyday kufuneka (it is necessary).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: The mountain is visible from afar.
drills.hint: to see flipped stative + present, class 9

drills.translation: It is necessary that we study.
drills.hint: to want flipped stative = be necessary

drills.translation: These books are available at the shop.
drills.hint: to get flipped stative + present, class 10
to throw-away flipped stative + perfect, class 7

drills.translation: It is evident that you are tired.
drills.hint: to see flipped stative + present, it-subject

points.title: Past continuous (bendifunda, ebetya)
points.explanation:
The be- auxiliary fuses with the concord for past-in-progress: bendifunda (I was studying), ubufunda (you were), ebetya (he was eating), besimamela (we were listening), bebedlala (they were playing).

Ubuphi? — where were you?

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: I was reading a book yesterday.
He was eating when I arrived.
We were listening to music at night.
I was asleep when you phoned.
The mothers were shopping at the market.

points.title: Purpose: ukuze + subjunctive
points.explanation: Ukuze opens a purpose clause and the verb ends subjunctive -e: funda ukuze uphumelele (study so that you pass). Negative purpose slips in -nga-: ukuze kungangeni umoya (so that the wind doesn't come in).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: Study hard so that you pass.
Study well so that you pass the exams.
We wake early so that we arrive on time.
I call you so that you help me.
Close the door so that the wind doesn't come in.
They work hard so that the children can study.

points.title: Because: kuba, ngoba — and kutheni (why)
points.explanation: Kuba and ngoba both open because-clauses; kutheni … nje asks why. Andiyi esikolweni kuba ndiyagula (I'm not going to school because I'm ill); kutheni ulate nje? (why are you late?).

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: I am not going to school because I am ill.
We stayed in the house because it is raining.
She is loved because she is kind.
I am happy because you have arrived.

points.title: Ideophones with ukuthi (wathi cwaka)
points.explanation:
Xhosa ideophones ride the verb -thi (say/do): wathi cwaka (went dead silent), wathi gqi (appeared suddenly), wathi tu (said nothing at all), zathi saa (scattered), lathi qatha (popped into mind).

The ideophone carries the image; -thi carries the grammar.
points.culture_note: Ideophones are performed — pitch, length, and a gesture are part of the word. Native reviewers should confirm regional forms.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: He appeared suddenly at the door.
I asked him; he said nothing at all.
The chickens scattered across the yard.
His name popped into my mind.
They were astonished when they saw the snake.

points.title: Amaqhalo — proverbs and ubuntu
points.function: Speak with the weight of the elders
points.explanation: Amaqhalo compress the concord grammar you know into fixed wisdom, starting with the most famous sentence in the language: umntu ngumntu ngabantu — a person is a person through people. Quoting the right one grants instant standing.
points.culture_note: Ukwanda kwaliwa ngumthakathi is said to thank someone: may your generosity keep increasing — only a witch would oppose it.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: A person is a person through people.
A bird builds with another bird's feathers.
A man's beauty is his cattle.
The buffalo is asked about from those who are ahead.
Increase is begrudged only by the witch.

points.title: Hlonipha — the respect register
points.explanation:
Respect is grammatical: elders are greeted in the plural (molweni), addressed as tata/mama rather than by name, and treated ngembeko (with respect).

Traditional hlonipha goes further — a makoti avoids her in-laws' names entirely, and camagu answers in ritual settings.
points.culture_note: Hlonipha is a living system, strongest in rural areas and ritual life — a native reviewer should tune these to the community the app serves.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: 'Greetings (respectful plural), father,' said the young man politely.
drills.hint: the plural greeting used to one elder

drills.translation: The bride respects (avoids) the names of her husband's parents.
We say 'father' when addressing an old man.
drills.hint: the respectful address for a man

drills.translation: When giving ritual thanks in a homestead, we say 'Camagu!'
It is not acceptable to call an elder by name.

points.title: Discourse particles (ke, nje, phofu, kaloku)
points.function: Carry the tone of a real conversation
points.explanation: Ke moves things along ('then/so'), nje softens ('just'), phofu adds an aside ('mind you'), kaloku appeals to shared sense ('you see / after all'), andithi tags for agreement ('isn't it?'), mhlawumbi hedges ('perhaps').

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: Mind you, he knows nothing about this.
You see, we are all still learning.

points.title: Greetings in full (molo, hamba kakuhle)
points.function: Open and close encounters like a local
points.explanation:
Molo one-to-one, molweni to many or to an elder; unjani? — ndiphilile, enkosi.

Partings split by who moves: hamba kakuhle (go well) to the one leaving, sala kakuhle (stay well) to the one staying. Uxolo excuses, enkosi kakhulu thanks warmly.

references.title: Live Lingua — Peace Corps Xhosa course

drills.translation: 'How are you?' 'I am well, thank you.'

drills.hint: the farewell to the one LEAVING
the farewell to the one STAYING

drills.translation: Thank you very much for your help.
